coredata.dc:description: In this work, Fe-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) were synthesized using o-phenylenediamine as a precursor and FeCl3·6H2O as a metal dopant by a one-step hydrothermal method. Notably, Fe-doping not only enhances the fluorescence of CDs, but also causes a red-shift in the emission wavelength. More importantly, Fe-CDs exhibit responsive fluorescence quenching to Cu2+ with high selectivity, sensitivity and anti-interference ability, enabling the sensor to achieve a detection limit of 0.23 μM for Cu2+. The quenching mechanism is triggered by the synergistic sensing process of dynamic and static quenching. Additionally, doping Fe into CDs can effectively regulate the electron density distribution and energy gap of the CDs, significantly altering their surface active sites. This coupling effect further augments the activity of the urea oxidation reaction (UOR). When utilized to catalyze UOR, Fe-CDs can achieve a battery voltage of 1.39 V at a current density of 100 mA cm−2 and demonstrate excellent 24-h stability. Therefore, Fe-CDs are a promising catalyst and have great significance in related fields such as energy conversion and urea treatment.

head.ce:title: Fe-doped carbon dots with enhanced fluorescence: Facilitating Cu2+ detection and urea electro-oxidation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, Fe-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) were synthesized using o-phenylenediamine as a precursor and FeCl3·6H2O as a metal dopant by a one-step hydrothermal method. Notably, Fe-doping not only enhances the fluorescence of CDs, but also causes a red-shift in the emission wavelength. More importantly, Fe-CDs exhibit responsive fluorescence quenching to Cu2+ with high selectivity, sensitivity and anti-interference ability, enabling the sensor to achieve a detection limit of 0.23 μM for Cu2+. The quenching mechanism is triggered by the synergistic sensing process of dynamic and static quenching. Additionally, doping Fe into CDs can effectively regulate the electron density distribution and energy gap of the CDs, significantly altering their surface active sites. This coupling effect further augments the activity of the urea oxidation reaction (UOR). When utilized to catalyze UOR, Fe-CDs can achieve a battery voltage of 1.39 V at a current density of 100 mA cm−2 and demonstrate excellent 24-h stability. Therefore, Fe-CDs are a promising catalyst and have great significance in related fields such as energy conversion and urea treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cu is a vital trace element for human body and plays a crucial role in numerous essential physiological processes of the nervous system [1,2]. However, excessive Cu intake can lead to developing neurodegenerative diseases including Parkinson's and Alzheimer's [3,4]. To safeguard human health, the World Health Organization (WHO) has set a limit on the concentration of Cu ions in drinking water to 31.5 μM [5,6]. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop an efficient Cu2+ detection technology. Currently, the main methods for detecting Cu2+ include ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrophotometry [7], atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [8], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [9], and electrochemical method [10]. Although these methods can determine Cu2+ content rapidly and precisely, they respectively have issues such as limited selectivity and sensitivity in UV–vis spectrophotometry, time-consuming and complex sample pretreatment in AAS [5], polyatomic ion interferences and high cost in ICP-MS, as well as electrode stability problems in the electrochemical method. In contrast, the fluorescence sensing detection method based on carbon dots (CDs) has become a prominent candidate in the field of ion detection. This is predominantly attributed to the remarkable luminescent characteristics of CDs, as well as the inherent advantages of this method, namely simple operation, high sensitivity, and excellent selectivity [11]. Notably, not all CDs-based methods share these merits. For example, Diraviam Jeeva et al. [12] prepared CDs from pumpkin seeds through hydrothermal carbonization, and further applied them as a dual-channel ratiometric fluorescent sensor for Cu2+ and Fe3+ sensing. However, Cu2+ and Fe3+ are prone to mutual interference, resulting in low selectivity for Cu2+ detection. Zhu et al. [13] fabricated double-emitting CDs via a one-pot hydrothermal method for the purpose of detecting Cu2+, but it has a relatively narrow linear range (2–20 μM) compared to other fluorescent sensors. Accordingly, although considerable advances have been made in using CDs to detect Cu2+, there are still limitations in terms of sensitivity and selectivity. Developing a convenient, accurate, and easy-to-use sensing platform based on CDs that can quickly respond to Cu2+ remains crucial for monitoring water safety. Noticeably, metal-doped carbon dots (CDs) have drawn attention as a promising material for sensing applications. The doping of metals can enhance the fluorescence properties of CDs, leading to improved sensitivity and selectivity in detecting various analytes.

Additionally, directly discharging untreated urea wastewater not only causes eutrophication of the water body, but also brings serious pollution to the ecosystem [14]. Compared to complex and energy-consuming traditional methods, energy-saving electrolytic urea technology is considered as a green and effective approach and has received increasing attention [15]. The complete urea electrolysis process can be expressed as CO(NH2)2 + H2O → 3H2 + N2 + CO2, which includes the anodic urea oxidation reaction (UOR, CO(NH2)2 + 6OH− → N2 + 5H2O) and the cathodic hydrogen evolution reaction (HER, 6H2O + 6e− → 3H2 + 6OH−) [16]. More importantly, the thermodynamic oxidation potential of UOR (0.37 V) is considerably lower than that of oxygen evolution reaction (OER) by electrolysis of water (1.23 V). It can not only purify urea-rich wastewater, but also produce hydrogen with high efficiency and low energy consumption [17,18]. However, due to the complex 6e− transfer process in the UOR reaction, efficient catalysts are required for urea electrolysis. CDs are regarded as excellent electron donors or electron acceptors, which can be employed as the catalyst support owing to their excellent stability, good conductivity, and low toxicity. When contrasted with non-metallic atoms, transition metal ions generally have more electrons, a greater number of free orbitals, and a larger atomic radius [19]. It has been shown that doping transition metals into CDs can effectively regulate the electron density distribution and energy gap of CDs, accelerate electron transfer in reactions, significantly alter their surface active sites, and thus improve their electrocatalytic performance[20–22]. Given the abundance and low toxicity of iron sources in transition metal elements, it is worth exploring Fe-doped CDs as potential catalysts to improve urea electrolysis [23].

Herein, Fe-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) were prepared by using o-phenylenediamine (OPD) as the carbon source and nitrogen source, and FeCl3 as the metal dopant. The optical characteristics of the as-prepared Fe-CDs were compared with those of undoped CDs prepared in the same conditions. Additionally, this work is dedicated to exploring the potential applications of Fe-CDs in two aspects. Firstly, we aim to explore the highly selective and sensitive detection of Cu2+ ions based on fluorescence quenching, along with delving into the detection mechanism. Secondly, we will investigate the catalytic behavior of Fe-CDs in alkaline electrolytes containing urea and compare it with that of undoped CDs. Through these efforts, we hope to contribute to the development of carbon-based nanocomposites as efficient catalysts for urea electrolysis, expand the scope of hydrogen production technology, and promote sustainable environmental development.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of Fe-CDs-x and c-CDs-x
ce:section.ce:para: In this project, a series of Fe-CDs-x and c-CDs-x were synthesized by the "one-pot solvothermal synthesis method" with varying heating time (The detailed description about the preparation and naming of these materials was provided in the Supplementary Material. Specifically, the difference between Fe-CDs-x and c-CDs-x lies in that FeCl3 is added during the preparation of Fe-CDs-x, where x = 1, 2, 3, 4 corresponds to heating times of 1 h, 3 h, 6 h, and 10 h, respectively. As the heating time increases, the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of Fe-CDs-x shifted from 420/510 nm to 470/585 nm, accompanied by a color change from green to orange (Fig. 1 a and b). To determine whether the observed redshift was due to the presence of Fe, a control experiment without doping Fe was conducted under the same conditions. The results showed that as the heating time increased from 1 h to 10 h, the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of c-CDs-x presented an inconspicuous change (420/512 nm to 420/530 nm), and the fluorescence color only displayed green to yellow-green. More importantly, the fluorescence intensity was significantly lower than that of the corresponding Fe-CDs-x. Moreover, Fe-CDs-4 presented a higher QY of 51.7 % compared to c-CDs-4 (34.2 %). This suggests that the addition of Fe may have altered the structure and surface state of CDs, resulting in an increase in QY. Overall, the addition of Fe resulted in a significant enhancement and redshift of fluorescence, as well as a shift in the peak position, in terms of both fluorescence intensity and luminescence color.

The UV–vis spectrum demonstrated that Fe-CDs-x and c-CDs-x exhibited a pronounced absorption peak at around 270 nm, which can be attributed to the π-π∗ transition caused by the CC bonds in the aromatic ring [24]. Additionally, the absorption peak near 420 nm may be due to the n-π∗ transition, which might be related to the electron transitions of the CO or CN bond [25,26]. The initial decrease in the 420 nm absorption peak intensity with the increase heating time (1–6 h) could be caused by the disruption of the CO or CN bond structure due to thermal effects or particle aggregation, while the subsequent increase might result from the reformation or new formation of CO or CN bonds with more favorable electronic configurations during further heating (10 h), or the generation of other species with similar n-π∗ transition characteristics that enhances the overall absorption at this wavelength (Fig. 1c, black line). Remarkably, the fluorescence emission peaks remained unchanged when the excitation wavelengths were changed, indicating an excitation-independent emission property (Fig. 1c and d). It must be noted that the Fe-CDs-4 exhibited strong orange fluorescence, which was further named Fe-CDs. Meanwhile, c-CDs-4 was further named c-CDs. These two representative samples were stored at 4 °C for subsequent experiments.

Furthermore, the emission properties of Fe-CDs and c-CDs were investigated in various solvents. When Fe-CDs were dispersed in DMSO, DMF, THF and PE sequentially, they produced red, orange, green, and blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light, respectively (Fig. S1a). As the solvent polarity increased, the maximum emission peak shifted towards longer wavelengths, progressing from blue to red on the spectrum (Figs. S1b and c). This phenomenon may be due to the formation of hydrogen bond complexes between the surface functional groups of Fe-CDs and solvent molecules. When the polarity of the solvent increases, its effect on the distribution of functional groups on the surface of Fe-CDs also increases. During this process, the excited state can be more effectively stabilized, and electrons are more likely to transfer to lower energy levels, ultimately leading to a red shift in photoluminescence (PL) emission [27]. The UV–Vis absorption spectra (Fig. S1f) revealed a significant red-shift in the absorption wavelengths of Fe-CDs with the increase in solvent polarity, confirming the effect of strong solvent polarity on the n-π∗ transition and resulting in a red-shift of the emission spectra [28]. However, c-CDs showed no significant dependence on the solvent (Figs. S1d and e), indicating their stable electronic structures and resistance to the influence of solvent molecules.

In addition to real-time and rapid detection, it is essential to guarantee the stable operation of the probe in various extreme environments (Fig. S2). As shown in Fig. S2a, following 2 h of UV irradiation, the fluorescence intensity of Fe-CDs exhibited minimal change, demonstrating excellent resistance to photobleaching. As the salt concentration increased, the fluorescence intensity of Fe-CDs remained largely unchanged (Fig. S2b), indicating its suitability for high-salt environments. Fig. S2c shows that Fe-CDs maintained consistent fluorescence intensity over a wide temperature range, demonstrating excellent thermal stability and retaining its structure and performance at elevated temperatures. Fig. S2d shows the fluorescence intensity change of Fe-CDs from pH 2 to 10. The intensity drops notably in acidic or alkaline conditions and peaks at pH 7. The instability in such solutions may be due to H+-induced protonation or OH−-induced deprotonation of Fe-CDs' surface functional groups, disrupting their structure and electronic configuration and thus reducing fluorescence intensity [5]. These findings confirm that Fe-CDs possess remarkable photostability and high environmental tolerance, making it a novel fluorescent probe with practical application potential.

The characterization of the morphology and size of Fe-CDs and c-CDs was carried out by TEM. The TEM images demonstrated that both Fe-CDs (Fig. 2 a) and c-CDs (Fig. 2d) exhibit good dispersion. HR-TEM images demonstrated clear lattice fringes for both Fe-CDs and c-CDs, with spacings of 0.25 nm (Figs. 2b) and 0.21 nm (Fig. 2e), respectively, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphite [21]. Furthermore, the histogram presenting the particle size distribution revealed that the average particle size of Fe-CDs is 2.0 nm (Fig. 2c), which is slightly larger than that of c-CDs (1.6 nm) (Fig. 2f). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns show diffraction peaks at 22°, 25°, 28.5°, and 43° (Fig. 2g), where the 22° peak likely represents a dominant local ordered structure, the 25° peak may indicate the (002) plane of a graphitic-like structure suggesting graphitization [13,29], the 28.5° and 43° peaks, probably related to the (101) plane, imply the existence of ordered crystalline regions [30], collectively suggesting a complex structure with a combination of ordered and disordered parts in the CDs [31]. However, the weaker peaks of Fe-CDs compared to c-CDs suggest that the degree of amorphous morphology of Fe-CDs is more significant than that of c-CDs.

The FT-IR spectra in Fig. 2h revealed the characteristic absorption peaks near 3460 cm−1, 2930 cm−1, 1670 cm−1, 1500 cm−1, and 1090 cm−1 for c-CDs and Fe-CDs, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of O–H/N–H, C–H, CN, CC, and C–O groups [26,32,33], respectively. In addition, at 3460 cm−1, the peak position of Fe-CDs is lower than that of c-CDs, indicating that the introduction of Fe may cause changes in the structure of O–H/N–H. Moreover, Fe-CDs displayed a unique characteristic absorption peak near 578 cm−1 which corresponded to the stretching vibration of the O–Fe–O bond [34]. As illustrated in Fig. 2i, the Raman spectrum displays two pronounced peaks at 1380 cm−1 and 1530 cm−1, which correspond to graphite (G-band) and disordered (D-band) carbon structures, respectively [35]. By calculating the ID/IG ratio, it was found that for Fe-CDs it is 0.89 and for c-CDs it is 0.92, indicating a reduction in surface defects and an increase in the graphitization degree of Fe-CDs [36].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was utilized to analyze the elemental composition and valence state of c-CDs and Fe-CDs. The full XPS spectrum of Fe-CDs (Fig. 3 a) shows four distinctive peaks at 285, 400, 532 and 711 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s, O1s and Fe2p, respectively. The percentages of C, O, N and Fe are found to be 79.91 %, 10.06 %, 8.47 % and 1.57 %, respectively. These results indicate that Fe element has been successfully incorporated into Fe-CDs compared to c-CDs. Table S1 shows the ratios of various chemical bonds in Fe-CDs and c-CDs, as determined by peak area. The high-resolution C 1s spectra illustrated in Fig. 3b and f, can be further decomposed into three distinct binding energy peaks, namely CC/C–C (284.7 eV), C–O/C–N (285.6 eV) and CO/CN (288.7 eV) [33]. From c-CDs to Fe-CDs, the CC/C–C peak is observed to decrease, which may be attributed to the formation of stronger chemical bonds between Fe and C. Conversely, the peaks of C–O/C–N and CO/CN peaks exhibited a slight increase, indicating an enhancement in the degree of sp3 hybridization and the carbonyl carbon content, resulting in a transformation of the internal structure of c-CDs [26]. As shown in Fig. 3c and g, the N1s spectra can be divided into three distinct peaks at 398.6 eV (pyridine N), 399.3 eV (pyrrole N) and 400.5 eV (graphite N). Table S1 demonstrates a notable decline in the proportion of pyridine N in Fe-CDs compared to c-CDs. The reduction of pyridine N content might lead to alterations in the electronic structure and energy level distribution of carbon dots. The doping of graphene N introduces impurity energy levels into its bandgap, thereby reducing the transition energy of electrons [37]. The O1s XPS spectra (Fig. 3d and h) exhibit two distinct peaks at 531.9 and 532.9 eV, corresponding to CO and C–O, respectively. The CO content rises from 64.15 % to 76.87 %, indicating an increase in the degree of surface oxidation of Fe-CDs [38]. As shown in Fig. 3e, the Fe 2p spectrum presents two sets of peaks at approximately 711.4 and 724.6 eV. These peaks correspond to the photoemission of the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 structures, respectively. Additionally, the high-resolution spectrum of Fe2p reveals four distinct peaks, which are ascribed to Fe2+ (710.3 and 723.2 eV) and Fe3+ (712.3 and 725.3 eV). This provides evidence for the coexistence of Fe3+ and Fe2+ on the surface of Fe-CDs [39]. The XPS and optical characterization results of Fe-CDs and c-CDs suggest that doping with iron promotes an increase in degree of sp3 hybridization, enhances the content of graphite nitrogen and surface CO groups, which has a synergistic effect on the structure and optical properties of Fe-CDs.

Through the above analysis of the characterization results, we can further assume that the red-shifted emission of Fe-CDs is mainly due to the combined effects of more CO groups and graphitic nitrogen in the Fe-CDs than c-CDs. The strong polarity of CO groups can modify the local electronic environment around the CDs, reducing the energy difference of electron transitions and causing a red shift in the emission spectrum. As a dopant, graphitic nitrogen can introduce new electronic states and change the band structure of the CDs, also leading to emission red shift. What's more, higher graphitization degree can expand the conjugated system of the CDs, facilitating electron delocalization. This delocalization lowers the energy of electron transitions, another key factor for the PL red shift. These factors may interact with each other, jointly influencing the red-shift of the emission spectrum of Fe-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specific detection of Cu2+ by Fe-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The surface of Fe-CDs is modified with numerous functional groups, rendering them suitable for detecting metal ions via fluorescence [40]. In Fig. 4 a and c, the fluorescence emission spectra of Fe-CDs, either without (blank) and with different metal ions, are displayed, along with the associated relative fluorescence ratios ((F/F0, where F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensities of Fe-CDs in the presence and absence of ions, respectively, under an excitation wavelength of 470 nm). Evidently, only the addition of Cu2+ significantly reduces the fluorescence intensity of Fe-CDs compared to other ions. It should be noted that MnO4 − also has a slightly stronger quenching effect on Fe-CDs, which may be due to its strong oxidative capacity and its own distinct color. However, it was found that adding Mn2+ did not have a significant quenching effect. Consequently, MnO4 − can be converted to Mn2+ by adding a reducing agent. Furthermore, common anions have a nearly negligible impact on the fluorescence intensity of Fe-CDs (Fig. 4b and c). Obviously, under the UV lamp (365 nm) (Fig. 4d and e), the fluorescence quenching phenomenon of Cu2+ on Fe-CDs can be clearly observed with the naked eye. To determine its suitability for actual samples, the anti-interference capacity of this fluorescence sensor was also investigated. The results demonstrate the interfering substances had almost no influence for Fe-CDs on the responsiveness of Cu2+ (Fig. 4f), indicating that Fe-CDs, as a fluorescence probe, possess effective anti-interference properties for Cu2+ detection.

On this basis, the sensitivity of Fe-CDs for determining Cu2+ was further investigated. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the emission peak intensity of Fe-CDs at 580 nm gradually decreases with the increase of Cu2+ concentration. Fig. 5b shows a partially enlarged image of the fluorescence intensity change of Fe-CDs in the Cu2+ concentration range of 1–10 μM. Fig. 5c illustrates the fluorescence intensity ratio F0/F of Fe-CDs with different concentration of Cu2+. Fig. 5d and e further clarify the relationship between the fluorescence change ratio (F0/F) of Fe-CDs and the Cu2+ concentration, establishing linear relationships between 10 - 100 μM and 1–10 μM, respectively. The fitting equations are:

Based on 3σ/k, the lowest detection limit (LOD) is 0.23 μM. It is noteworthy that this LOD is beneath the maximum allowable concentration of Cu2+ in drinking water as defined by the WHO (31.5 μM), indicating its excellent sensing performance in the detection of Cu2+. Compared with other sensors in Table S2, our sensor exhibits a broader linear range and lower detection limit. In addition, the quenching phenomenon of Fe-CDs can also be observed with the naked eye (Fig. 5d inset). The intensity of the orange fluorescence shows a gradual decline in conjunction with the incremental elevation in Cu2+ concentration, demonstrating the reliability and sensitivity of the detection process. The fluorescence quenching effect may be attributed to the formation of electrostatic interactions of Cu2+ captured by the surface groups of Fe-CDs, promoting electron transfer between Fe-CDs and Cu2+, and coordination to form complexes [41] (Fig. 5f). The energy of the fluorescence emission will be absorbed, thus resulting in the fluorescence quenching effect.

The concentration of Cu2+ in actual water samples was determined by employing the standard addition method. Table S3 presents a summary of the research results, showing average recovery rates of 92.5 %, 91.42 %, and 99.3 % in tap water. Moreover, the relative standard deviation (RSD%) of this method is less than 1.5 %. The results demonstrate that Fe-CDs is a novel fluorescence probe that can accurately and reliably determine Cu2+ in actual water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching mechanism of Fe-CDs induced by Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: For the purpose of attaining a deeper comprehension of the fluorescence quenching mechanism of Cu2+ in relation to Fe-CDs, changes in fluorescence lifetime, ultraviolet and infrared absorption spectra, and fluorescence intensity at different temperatures were investigated. The corresponding fluorescence decay curves are presented in Fig. 6 a. The fluorescence lifetimes (τavg) of Fe-CDs were calculated to be 7.17 ns, 6.72 ns, and 4.72 ns in the absence of Cu2+, with 10 μM and 100 μM Cu2+, respectively (Table S4). The addition of the quencher led to a decrease in the fluorescence lifetime, suggesting that the fluorescence quenching process was dynamic quenching which was brought about by the collision between the excited state of the fluorescent group and the quencher [42].

The UV–Vis absorption spectra (Fig. 6b) reveal that after adding 10 μM Cu2+, the position and shape of the UV absorption peak remain basically unchanged. However, adding a higher concentration of Cu2+(100 μM) makes the absorption peak at 480 nm stronger and undergoes a blue shift. This indicates that the fluorescence quenching of Fe-CDs caused by Cu2+ is mainly dynamic quenching at a lower concentration of Cu2+, but it mainly transforms into static quenching, especially at a higher Cu2+ concentration [43]. It is speculated that this phenomenon may be due to the strong binding between hydroxyl, amino, carboxyl groups on Fe-CDs and Cu2+, leading to electron or energy transfer [44]. Cu2+, belonging to the transition group element with an unfilled d orbital, tends to absorb lone pair electrons of oxygen, nitrogen and other atoms from the surface of Fe-CDs. Thus, it is prone to form stable coordination compounds with Fe-CDs, and this tendency is even more prominent at a higher concentration of Cu2+ [42]. In the FT-IR spectrum, the characteristic absorption peak of O–H/N–H at around 3500 cm−1 is enhanced (Fig. S3), indicating a chemical coordination effect between Fe-CDs and Cu2+, which provides further support for the static quenching mechanism [45].

Subsequently, cyclic voltammetry was used to explore the electrochemical properties of Fe-CDs (Fig. 6c). The highest occupied (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied (LUMO) energy levels of Fe-CDs were calculated using the following empirical formulas [46]:
E HOMO
= − e
(
E ox
+ 4.4
)
E LUMO
= − e
(
E red
+ 4.4
)
Here, Eox and Ered represent the oxidation potential and reduction potential of Fe-CDs, respectively. The inset is for determining the indirect band gap Eg of Fe-CDs, Fe-CDs +10 μM Cu2+, and Fe-CDs +100 μM Cu2+ by plotting the curve of (αhυ)2 against hυ based on the ultraviolet absorption spectrum [38]. The Ered was determined to be −0.24 eV by cyclic voltammetry (Fig. 6d), and the corresponding ELUMO value was calculated to be −4.16 eV. The optical band gap (Eg) of Fe-CDs was calculated to be 4.3 eV from the ultraviolet spectrum. According to the formula: EHOMO = ELUMO - Eg, the EHOMO of Fe-CDs was −8.46 eV. In addition, using the same method, for Fe-CDs containing 10 μM Cu2+, Ered and Eg were calculated to be −0.30 eV and 4.31 eV, respectively (Fig. 6e), and ELUMO and EHOMO were −4.10 eV and −8.41 eV, respectively; for Fe-CDs containing 100 μM Cu2+, Ered and Eg were −0.34 eV and 4.34 eV (Fig. 6f), and ELUMO and EHOMO were −4.06 eV and −8.4 eV, respectively. The elevation in the EHOMO energy level rendered Fe-CDs more prone to losing electrons and more likely to undergo oxidation reactions with Cu2+. As shown in the schematic diagram of the energy excitation mechanism of Fe-CDs in Fig. 6g, as the concentration of Cu2+ rises, it tends to combine with Fe-CDs to form a strong coordination compound of Cu2+/Fe-CDs. This process promotes the HUMO energy level of Fe-CDs to move towards the HUMO energy level of Cu2+, thereby enhancing the selectivity of detection.

The Stern-Volmer equation (F0/F = 1 + Ksv[C] = 1 + Kq⋅τ0[C]) was further employed to investigate the quenching efficiency [47]. In this equation, F0 stands for the fluorescence intensity acquired when the quencher is absent, while F represents the fluorescence intensity obtained when the quencher is present. Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, [C] denotes the concentration of the quencher, Kq is the bimolecular quenching rate constant, and τ0 is the average lifetime of the fluorescent molecule without the quencher. As temperature rises, the dynamic quenching constant rises, while the static quenching constant falls [44]. In Fig. 6h, there is a good linear relationship between F0/F and [C], and the value of Ksv can be obtained from the slope of the straight line. Kq was calculated from the Stern-Volmer formula. As illustrated in Table S5, Ksv diminishes with rising temperature, aligning with the static quenching mechanism [46]. In conclusion, the quenching mechanism of Fe-CDs can be attributed to the combination of dynamic and static quenching processes. This phenomenon has also been observed in previous studies [48–50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic performance of Fe-CDs for UOR
ce:section.ce:para: Since the urea content in human urine is 2.0–2.5 wt% (corresponding to a molar concentration of 0.33 M), 0.33 M urea was chosen as the electrolyte. The performance comparison between c-CDs and Fe-CDs was presented in Fig. 7 a. When the electrolyte solution was 1.0 M KOH and 0.33 M urea, the UOR of Fe-CDs and c-CDs required 1.39 V and 1.86 V, respectively, to obtain a current density of 100 mA cm−2. The performance of Fe-CDs is significantly better than that of c-CDs, indicating that Fe doping significantly improves the catalytic activity of UOR. The UOR activity of Fe-CDs was primarily evaluated in 1.0 M KOH and 0.33 M urea. As depicted in Fig. 7b, Fe-CDs displayed an initial potential of 1.35 V for UOR, which is considerably lower than that of OER (1.61 V). To attain a high current density of 100 mA cm−2, the UOR potential of Fe-CDs was merely 1.39 V, whereas OER demands a potential of 1.82 V. This finding indicates that substituting OER with UOR can significantly reduce the potential, thus decreasing energy consumption. In addition, we investigated the electrocatalytic activity of the Fe-CDs electrocatalyst at different pH levels, as shown in Fig. 7c. It was found that when the KOH concentration was increased from 0.1 M to 1.0 M, the activity was enhanced and the j-value gradually increased. This is mainly attributed to the increase of OH− ions at the electrode/electrolyte interface, which leads to enhanced conductivity, indicating that the UOR process is significantly affected by the amount of KOH. The effect of urea concentration (Fig. 7d) on the UOR performance was also evaluated in aqueous KOH solution (1.0 M). The j-value increased significantly with increasing urea concentration and reached the maximum value at a urea concentration of 0.33 M, indicating that the introduction of urea may accelerate the charge transfer rate of the catalyst. The charge transfer during the catalytic reaction was analyzed using electrochemical impedance (Fig. 7e), and the smallest Nyquist semicircle indicates that Fe-CDs has the best electrical conductivity. The Tafel slope is another kinetic parameter for examining the catalytic activity of the UOR catalyst. The smaller the Tafel slope, the better the electron transfer of the UOR catalyst and the higher the electrocatalytic performance [23].Within the same voltage gradient range, the Tafel slope of Fe-CDs is 23.03 mV dec−1, which is lower than the Tafel slope of c-CDs (94.47 mV dec−1) (Fig. 7f), indicating that Fe-CDs have faster reaction kinetics in the UOR process. Fig. 7g depicts the schematic diagram of the electrochemical oxidation of urea, which results in the generation of the gas products CO2 and N2 as the consequence of the reaction. In comparison with recently reported transition metal-based electrocatalysts (Fig. 7h & Table S6), Fe-CDs demonstrates superior urea oxidation (UOR) activity within an alkaline urea solution[51–60].

At the same time, the electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) of the synthesized sample was determined. CV curves with different scan rates are plotted in the non-Faraday range (0.82–0.92 V) (Fig. 8 a and d). ECSA can be calculated using the formula ECSA = Cdl/Cs, where Cdl is the linear regression slope between the current density of the CV curve and different scanning rates, and Cs is the capacitance per unit area of a given electrode material [61]. The Cdl of c-CDs was 2.04 mF cm−2, while the Cdl of Fe-CDs was 2.61 mF cm−2 (Fig. 8b and e). Correspondingly, the ECSA values of c-CDs and Fe-CDs were 51 and 65.25, respectively. Evidently, the larger the Cdl, the larger the ECSA, which indicates that more catalytically active centers are exposed. Fe-CDs exhibited excellent UOR kinetics, which can be attributed to the intramolecular charge transfer induced by Fe-CDs, enhancing the oxidation effect of urea. Compared with c-CDs, the current fluctuation of Fe-CDs was not significant within 24 h of electrolysis at constant potential (0.6 V vs. RHE) (Fig. 8c and f). The results suggest that the electrode is stable, corrosion-resistant and suitable for urea electrolysis. Therefore, it can be concluded that Fe-CDs has better catalytic performance for urea than undoped c-CDs.

The electrocatalytic performance of Fe-CDs in practical applications was further investigated. We selected samples such as human urine (Fig. 9 a), seawater (Fig. 9b), and river water (Fig. 9c) from daily life, with 1.0 M KOH as supporting electrolytes in the solution samples. At a current density of 100 mA cm−2 (Fig. 9d), the initial voltage of Fe-CDs in 1.0 M KOH and human urine solution was 1.70 V, which was 0.31 V higher than that in 1.0 M KOH and 0.33 M urea solution (1.39 V), confirming the feasibility of Fe-CDs for human urine electrolysis. In addition, Fe-CDs also showed excellent catalytic performance in seawater and river water (Fig. 9e and f). At 100 mA cm−2, the initial potential values of seawater and river water (0.33 M urea with 1.0 M KOH) were both 1.77 V, with a positive shift of 0.38 V, and the catalytic performance was maintained at 97.8 %. At a constant voltage, Fe-CDs have been observed to catalyze stably in human urine, seawater, and river water (with 1.0 M KOH) for 24 h (Fig. 9g–i). They exhibited a largely unaltered potential distribution, indicating that the bubbles formed on the electrode surface remained stable throughout the catalytic process. The above series of results clearly indicated that Fe-CDs catalysts exhibit high performance electrocatalytic UOR activity when used in practical scenarios such as the treatment of human urine, seawater and river water, further proving its potential as UOR catalyst in practical application.

In conclusion, Fe-doped CDs (Fe-CDs) were successfully prepared with enhanced fluorescence by using o-phenylenediamine as the precursor and FeCl3·6H2O as the dopant. The synthesized Fe-CDs can be quenched by Cu2+ and employed as a fluorescence sensor for detecting Cu2+, exhibiting high selectivity and anti-interference capabilities with a detection limit of 0.23 μM, as well as preferable recovery rate in the detection of Cu2+ in actual water samples. Moreover, it has been shown that dynamic and static quenching play a synergistic role in the sensing process of Cu2+ by Fe-CDs. Additionally, Fe-CDs can serve as efficient catalysts for urea catalysis due to Fe doping, providing more catalytic active sites, improving its intrinsic activity and accelerating electron transfer, thus achieving the oxidation process of urea (electrolytic cell voltage up to 1.39 V at 100 mA cm−2 current density). More importantly, Fe-CDs can also maintain stable and efficient electrocatalytic performance in practical applications such as urine, seawater, river water and other water systems.
